questions.question_text: The most widely used residential purchase contract in Pennsylvania is published by:
questions.explanation: The PAR Standard Agreement of Sale (Form ASR) is the most commonly used residential purchase contract for 1-4 unit properties in Pennsylvania. It is published by the Pennsylvania Association of Realtors.

questions.question_text: Under Pennsylvania law, all agreements of sale must be:
questions.explanation: All agreements of sale must be in writing under the Statute of Frauds. While notarization is needed for deeds, the purchase agreement itself only needs to be in writing and signed by the parties.

questions.question_text: After an agreement of sale is signed, the broker must:
questions.correct_answer: Give copies to all parties at signing
questions.explanation: The broker must provide copies of the agreement to all parties at the time of signing. This ensures each party has immediate access to the executed contract terms.

questions.question_text: A PA agreement of sale must include all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Required elements include legal names, legal description, price, deposit, financing terms, settlement date, and title condition. A buyer's credit score is private financial information, not a required contract term.

questions.question_text: The PAR Standard Agreement of Sale (Form ASR) is designed for which type of property?
questions.explanation: Form ASR is designed for residential properties with 1-4 units. PAR has separate forms for new construction, commercial properties, and vacant land transactions.

questions.question_text: Does Pennsylvania provide a statutory attorney review period for purchase contracts?
questions.correct_answer: No, PA has no statutory attorney review
distractors: Yes, but only for first-time buyers
questions.explanation: Unlike New Jersey, Pennsylvania does NOT have a statutory attorney review period. There is no automatic right to void a signed contract for attorney review. However, parties may include an attorney review contingency.

questions.question_text: The Installment Land Contract Law in PA applies specifically in which counties?
questions.explanation: The Installment Land Contract Law (68 P.S. § 902 et seq.) applies in counties of the first class (Philadelphia) and second class (Allegheny). Other counties are not governed by this specific statute.

questions.question_text: Under a PA installment land contract, the buyer receives:
distractors: No title interest until final payment
questions.explanation: In an installment land contract, the buyer receives equitable title while the seller retains legal title until the final payment is made. The buyer has possession and use but cannot convey legal title.

questions.question_text: In Philadelphia County, an installment land contract must be recorded within how many days of execution?
questions.explanation: In applicable counties (Philadelphia and Allegheny), the seller must record the installment land contract within 10 days of execution. This recording requirement protects the buyer's equitable interest.

questions.question_text: Are attorneys required to be present at real estate closings in Pennsylvania?
questions.correct_answer: No, but they are commonly used in some counties
distractors: Yes, by state law in all transactions
No, and they are rarely involved
questions.explanation: Pennsylvania does not require an attorney at closing. Title companies conduct most closings. However, attorneys are commonly involved in some counties, and buyers or sellers may choose to retain counsel.

questions.question_text: Pennsylvania follows which type of recording statute?
questions.explanation: Pennsylvania is a race-notice state: a subsequent purchaser who records first AND had no notice of a prior unrecorded conveyance takes priority. Both recording speed and lack of notice matter.

questions.question_text: Buyer A purchases property but does not record. Later, Buyer B purchases the same property with no knowledge of Buyer A's deed and records immediately. Under PA law:
distractors: Buyer A has priority as first buyer
The seller decides who keeps the property
questions.explanation: Under PA's race-notice statute, Buyer B wins because they recorded first AND had no notice of Buyer A's prior purchase. If Buyer B had known about Buyer A's deed, Buyer A would prevail.

questions.question_text: Before a deed can be recorded in Pennsylvania, the grantor must pay:
questions.explanation: Pennsylvania requires the realty transfer tax to be paid before the deed can be recorded with the County Recorder of Deeds. This ensures tax collection at the time of property transfer.

questions.question_text: Deeds in Pennsylvania are recorded with the:
questions.explanation: Pennsylvania deeds are recorded at the County Recorder of Deeds office in the county where the property is located. Each of PA's 67 counties has its own recorder.

questions.question_text: At a PA settlement, the deed must be:
questions.correct_answer: Signed by the seller and notarized
distractors: Signed by both buyer and seller
questions.explanation: The deed must be signed by the grantor (seller) and notarized, then delivered to the buyer at settlement. The buyer does not sign the deed — they sign the mortgage document if financing.
